coredata.dc:title: F, N co-doped carbon nanomaterials for multi-level information encryption: Room-temperature phosphorescence and solid-state fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongmei
Guo, Xiangjun
Xu, Jiyao
Zhang, Yuman
Deng, Yujia
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: The non-radiative transition of triplet excitons limits the emission of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). In this paper, F, N co-doped carbon nanomaterials including carbon dot solutions (L-CDs) with cyan fluorescence and solid-state carbon nanowires (S-CNWs) with yellow fluorescence were successfully prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal method. Interestingly, L-CDs@PVA composites with green RTP were obtained by mixing L-CDs with PVA. The RTP production was attributed to F, N related heteroatomic functional groups on the surface of L-CDs. The C-N, CN bonds form lone electron pairs, which promote the electron transition from n→π∗. The F doping reduces the energy gap from the single to the triplet state, which facilitates the inter-system crossing (ISC) process and increases the radiative transition of the triplet exciton to produce RTP. In addition, PVA forms abundant hydrogen bonds with L-CDs to effectively immobilize the luminescence center and isolate atmospheric oxygen, so that L-CDs@PVA has stable phosphorescence emission at room temperature and this phosphorescence is reversible in dry and wet states. In contrast, S-CNWs displayed solid-state yellow fluorescence, but no phosphorescence even though doped into the PVA matrix (S-CNWs@PVA). Therefore, based on the different characteristics of fluorescence and phosphorescence of L-CDs and S-CNWs, a complex, multi-level information encryption method is proposed and the potential applications of S-CNWs powder for fingerprint recording are explored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: F, N co-doped
Carbon dots
Carbon nanowires
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Solid-state fluorescence
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA composites
Optical properties of L-CDs and S-CNWs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) TEM, HR-TEM and Fourier transform image of L-CDs; (d) size distribution histograms of L-CDs; (e) XRD spectra of ENR, L-CDs, and S-CNWs; (f) TEM image of S-CNWs; (g) elemental mapping distribution images of S-CNWs; (h) Raman spectra of L-CNWs and S-CNWs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of ENR, L-CDs, and S-CNWs; (b) full XPS spectra of L-CDs and S-CNWs; (c-f) high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s of L-CDs and S-CNWs, respectively.

(a) UV absorption curves (black), optimal excitation (red), and emission (blue) curves of L-CDs, inset: optical photographs of L-CDs solutions under daylight and UV irradiation; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of L-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (c) CIE coordinates of L-CDs; (d) UV absorption curves (black), optimal excitation (red) and emission (blue) curves of S-CNWs emission (blue) curves, insets: optical photographs of S-CNWs under daylight and UV irradiation; (e) fluorescence emission spectra of S-CNWs at different excitation wavelengths; (f) CIE coordinate diagrams of S-CNWs.

(a) Optimal excitation and optimal emission spectra of fluorescence and phosphorescence of L-CDs@PVA; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of L-CDs@PVA at different excitation wavelengths; (c) CIE coordinate diagram corresponding to the fluorescence emission of L-CDs@PVA; (d) phosphorescence emission spectra of L-CDs@PVA at different excitation wavelengths; (e) phosphorescence decay curve of L-CDs@PVA at 350 nm excitation; (f) luminescence mechanism diagram of L-CDs@PVA.

(a) Photographs of QR codes printed on filter paper by L-CDs@PVA in daylight, after turning on and off the 365 nm UV lamp; (b) optical photographs of encryption of letters; (c) photographs of binary code design with UV lamp turned on and off; (d) optical photographs of encryption of numbers and in dry and wet conditions (blue and red channels of b, c, d are occupied by L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA); (e) UV photographs of S-CNWs solid powder in both dry and wet conditions; (f) photographs and detail enlargements of S-CNWs recorded fingerprints.
Scheme diagram for the preparation of L-CDs and S-CNWs.

head.ce:title: F, N co-doped carbon nanomaterials for multi-level information encryption: Room-temperature phosphorescence and solid-state fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The non-radiative transition of triplet excitons limits the emission of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). In this paper, F, N co-doped carbon nanomaterials including carbon dot solutions (L-CDs) with cyan fluorescence and solid-state carbon nanowires (S-CNWs) with yellow fluorescence were successfully prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal method. Interestingly, L-CDs@PVA composites with green RTP were obtained by mixing L-CDs with PVA. The RTP production was attributed to F, N related heteroatomic functional groups on the surface of L-CDs. The C-N, CN bonds form lone electron pairs, which promote the electron transition from n→π∗. The F doping reduces the energy gap from the single to the triplet state, which facilitates the inter-system crossing (ISC) process and increases the radiative transition of the triplet exciton to produce RTP. In addition, PVA forms abundant hydrogen bonds with L-CDs to effectively immobilize the luminescence center and isolate atmospheric oxygen, so that L-CDs@PVA has stable phosphorescence emission at room temperature and this phosphorescence is reversible in dry and wet states. In contrast, S-CNWs displayed solid-state yellow fluorescence, but no phosphorescence even though doped into the PVA matrix (S-CNWs@PVA). Therefore, based on the different characteristics of fluorescence and phosphorescence of L-CDs and S-CNWs, a complex, multi-level information encryption method is proposed and the potential applications of S-CNWs powder for fingerprint recording are explored.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and easy surface modification [1–4], carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention of numerous researchers since they were first proposed in 2004 [5]. After more than a decade of continuous exploration and optimization, the good application properties of CDs have made them a rising material star in the fields of medical tumor therapy [6], energy conversion and storage [7], light-emitting devices [8], and detection [9]. As well, room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots enable more advanced applications than fluorescent CDs due to their dual emission properties of fluorescence and phosphorescence, such as providing sophisticated encryption procedures for information security [10,11]. In recent years, CDs have become a good choice for the preparation of RTP materials compared to pure organic compounds with high cytotoxicity and non-renewable rare earth-doped materials [12]. Moreover, unlike the complicated organic synthesis steps and tedious post-treatment [13,14], the hydrothermal method can synthesize CDs in one step simply and quickly. Phosphorescence emission is produced because a fraction of excitons in the lowest singly excited state S1 reaching the lowest triplet excited state T1 by inter-system crossing (ISC) and then transitioning to the ground state S0 [15]. However, these triplet excitons are susceptible to being consumed by the vibrational dissipation of atmospheric and molecular oxygen [16,17], which manifests itself macroscopically as quenching of the phosphorescence.

In order to improve the optical properties of CDs and to solve the problem that triplet excitons are easily consumed by oxygen molecules, heteroatoms such as B, N, and F [18–20] are usually doped into the carbon skeleton to change the internal chemical structure of CDs. Among them, the surface of the N atom functional group can form lone electron pairs to promote the n→π∗ transition in the system, and the F atom can provide spatial protection and reduce the energy gap of ISC [21]. Thus, F, N co-doping is more conducive to promote the exciton to go from single to triplet state. Alternatively, the CDs are dispersed in some matrix (polymer [22], zeolite [23], metal-organic framework [24], etc.) that hardens the phosphorescent centroid. Phosphor emission is achieved by forming a rigid environment to isolate the atmospheric oxygen and weaken the non-radiative decay and quenching process of triplet-state excitons. In most cases, RTP-CDs are synthesized by a combination of these two means to optimize the optical properties to the maximum extent and endow CDs with more widespread applications. Qu et al. [25] have prepared a series of flexible and transparent phosphorescent films by compounding Cl-doped CDs with oxygen-permeable polyvinylpyrrolidone, successfully realizing the grayscale display function of dynamic phosphorescent materials and providing a new idea for optical pattern anti-counterfeiting. In addition, the aggregation of CDs also affects their luminescence properties. Chen et al. [26] have synthesized yellow carbon dots with high quantum yields and realized white light electroluminescent devices with tunable color temperature by controlling the degree of aggregation of carbon dots, which has broad application prospects in new lighting fields. However, the self-assembly behavior of CDs from individual nanodots to large aggregates has rarely been studied. Therefore, the development of CDs with both self-assembly behavior and potential phosphorescent properties is of great research significance.

Enrofloxacin belongs to fluoroquinolones, which contain an aromatic ring structure and F and N atoms. The use of enrofloxacin as a precursor facilitates the formation of aromatic conjugate structures to increase the rigidity of CDs and provide a visible light response [27]. It also facilitates the functionalization of F and N atoms on the surface of CDs to form a large number of n→π∗ to promote ISC [28,29]. Therefore, this work successfully prepared F, N co-doped carbon nanomaterials using enrofloxacin. The pyrolysis product can be divided into two parts after being placed for a period of time. The supernatant, named L-CDs, were transparent solutions with yellowish color under sunlight and cyan fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light. At the same time, for the precipitation section, carbon nanowires (S-CNWs) with solid-state yellow fluorescence emission were obtained due to the self-assembly behavior of L-CDs with incompletely carbonized enrofloxacin ( Scheme 1). And the self-assembly behavior will stop when the concentration of surface functional groups of L-CDs and S-CNWs reached equilibrium in the solution. Furthermore, neither the L-CDs solution nor PVA possesses phosphorescence emission. Miraculously, the L-CDs@PVA composite obtained by dispersing L-CDs in the PVA matrix not only has cyan fluorescence but also a continuous green phosphorescence can be observed after the removal of the UV source, and its phosphorescence lifetime reached 584.51 ms. This is attributed to the protection of the long polymer chains of PVA and the rigid environment formed by the abundant hydrogen bonds, which effectively lock the emitting material and suppress the nonradiative decay of triplet excitons. And the doped F atoms improve the hydrogen bonding interactions and facilitate the stable emission of phosphorescence. Significantly, different from L-CDs, the composite of S-CNWs@PVA can only be observed with cyan fluorescence emission without the phosphorescence phenomenon. More interestingly, the S-CNWs possess solid-state yellow fluorescence. And it has distinctly different fluorescent colors under dry and moist conditions and is bi-directionally convertible. Therefore, taking advantage of the different luminescence behaviors of L-CDs and S-CNWs can convey different information under fluorescence and phosphorescence emission, respectively. It provides a simple and novel design method for multilevel information encryption.

Enrofloxacin (ENR) solution was purchased from Hefei Zhonglong Shenli Animal Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Hefei, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Fourier transform (FFT) images were observed on a JEM-1200EX transmission microscope (JOEL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were observed on a Regulus 8100 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Raman spectra were obtained on a DXR2 laser microscopical laser raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were obtained on a Smart Lab 3KW X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on a NICOLET 5700 spectrometer with KBr spheres (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H NMR) were obtained on a Bruker AVANCE III HD 400 MHz superconducting nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a TU-1901 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). Phosphor spectra, phosphor lifetimes (excitation wavelength of 350 nm), and fluorescence lifetimes (excitation wavelengths of 420 and 470 nm for L-CDs and S-CNWs tests, respectively) were performed on an Edinburgh FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a microsecond flash (μF) and time-selected pass.

L-CDs and S-CNWs were prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. A clarified yellow solution was obtained by placing 3 mL of enrofloxacin solution (0.025 g/mL) with 27 mL of ultrapure water in a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 6 h, and then cooled naturally to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm/min for 15 min and filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. The filtered solution was allowed to stand at 4 °C for 8 h and then flocculent precipitation was produced, which was transferred to an extraction funnel for separation to obtain light yellow transparent carbon dot solution (the supernatant) and yellow carbon nanowires (the precipitation), named L-CDs and S-CNWs, respectively. The S-CNWs were dried at 60 °C for 1 h, then ground to powder and stored in a sealed container at room temperature, and the carbon dot solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA composites
ce:section.ce:para: In a 200 mL beaker, 8 g of PVA and 100 mL of ultrapure water were added and heated in a water bath at 90 °C until the PVA was completely dissolved to obtain a PVA solution with a concentration of 8%. After that, 20 mg of S-CNWs powder was weighed and dispersed in 8 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 15 min 4 mL of L-CDs solution (2.5 mg/mL) and 4 mL of S-CNWs solution (2.5 mg/mL) were added to 12 mL of PVA solution, defoamed by ultrasonic treatment, and poured into plastic surface dishes. Then, dried under a vacuum at 60 °C for 10 h to obtain carbon nanomaterial-doped PVA composites, which were labeled as L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA, respectively.

To explore the reasons for the luminescence of L-CDs and S-CNWs, we analyzed the morphology and structure of L-CDs and S-CNWs. It is observed by TEM images ( Fig. 1a) that the L-CDs are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles without agglomeration and have well-resolved lattice stripes, indicating a high crystallinity of its carbon cores (Fig. 1b). The Fourier transform (FFT) (Fig. 1c) calculation of L-CD shows that its lattice stripe spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the graphite (100) crystal plane, indicating that L-CDs have a similar graphite structure[30]. And L-CDs with an average size of 2.99 nm (Fig. 1d). S-CNWs are very different from L-CDs in terms of morphology. The TEM (Fig. 1f) and SEM (Fig. S1a) images of S-CNWs show that S-CNWs are disordered stacked nanowires with diameters around 600 nm. Their elemental mapping (Fig. 1g and Fig. S1b) indicates that S-CNWs are composed of four elements, C, N, O, and F, and uniformly distributed on their surfaces. XRD spectra as shown in Fig. 1e display that ENR has distinct characteristic peaks at 9.13°, 20.84°, and 24.12°. While, L-CDs have a strong diffraction band centered at 2θ = 22.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating that the L-CDs have high crystallinity [31]. The strong diffraction peaks of S-CNWs appear at 7.09°, 9.67°, and 25.69°, and there is a weak diffraction band centered at 23.5° with an increased half-peak width, indicating that the graphitization is reduced considerably [32,33]. Typical Raman spectra (Fig. 1h) displays two peaks at 1365 and 1590 cm−1 for L-CDs and S-CNWs, corresponding to the disordered carbon domain (D-band) and sp2 conjugated domain (G-band) [34] of the carbon materials, respectively. Moreover, the ratio of IG/ID can assess the degree of graphitization of carbon materials. Here, the value of IG/ID of L-CDs (1.01) is significantly higher than S-CNWs (0.69), indicating a higher content of disordered carbon domains and a lower degree of graphitization in S-CNWs. This may be caused by the high doping content of F and N, which induces more surface defects [10,31,35].

Subsequently, FT-IR measurements were performed to understand the surface chemical structures of ENR, L-CDs, and S-CNWs. As shown in Fig. 2a, ENR, L-CDs, and S-CNWs have an absorption band at 3350–3500 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH/N-H and an absorption band at 3050–2850 cm−1 attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of C-H. The absorption peaks at 1725, 1640, and 1475 cm−1 are attributed to CN, CO, and CC stretching vibrations, and those at 1380, 1260, and 1165 cm−1 to C-N, C-O, and C-F stretching vibrations, respectively [20,21,36]. The results show that the L-CDs and S-CNWs have aromatic structures and are successfully doped with F and N atoms. In addition, it can be clearly observed that the C-H stretching and bending vibration absorption peaks of S-CNWs at 3050–2850 cm−1 are significantly stronger than those of L-CDs, indicating that the presence of a large number of carbon chains in S-CNWs, together with C-F bonds, increases the surface hydrophobicity of S-CNWs [37,38] and allows S-CNWs to precipitate out in aqueous solution after self-assembly. The structural details of ENRs, L-CDs, and S-CNWs were further analyzed by the measurement of 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2). In the range of 0.5–1.5 ppm correlate with the presence of cycloalkanes, signals in 2–4 ppm correspond to saturated aliphatic hydrocarbons -CH2 and in 7–9 ppm correspond to hydrogen atoms on benzene rings [34,39]. The results of XPS full scan spectra (Fig. 2b) show that both L-CDs and S-CNWs have four characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s. Among them, the contents of C, N, O, and F in L-CDs are 66.62%, 10.39%, 19.72%, and 3.27%, respectively, while the contents of C, N, O, and F in S-CNWs are 71.52%, 12.15%, 11.77%, and 4.55%, respectively (Table S1). The C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of L-CDs and S-CNWs (Fig. 2c) reveal four peaks at 284.4, 285.4, 286.3, and 288.0 eV belonging to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, CN/CO and C-F bonds, respectively [40,41]. The N1s high-resolution spectra of L-CDs and S-CNWs (Fig. 2d) reveal the presence of pyridinic-N (398.6 eV) and pyrrolic-N (400.2 eV) [42]. The O1s high-resolution spectra of L-CDs (Fig. 2e) are dissociated into three peaks, including CO, O-H/C-O, and OC-O at 530.2, 531.5, and 534.5 eV, respectively. While the O1s bands of S-CNWs exhibit only two peaks at 530.6 for CO and 532.1 eV for O-H/C-O [43]. The analysis of the F1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2f) shows that the F atoms in L-CDs are mainly in the form of semi-ionic C-F (686.6 eV) and covalent C-F (687.0 eV). While the F atoms in S-CNWs are three main forms, corresponding to semi-ionic C-F (686.4 eV), covalent C-F (687.0 eV), and -CF2 (687.8 eV), respectively [41,44]. These results not only confirm the successful doping of F and N atoms in L-CDs and S-CNWs but also demonstrate the material surface is modified by many functional groups. By comparing the contents of all the chemical bonds (Table S2), it is found that the biggest difference between them is the OC-O bond. It accounted for 11.3% of the O1s in L-CDs and 0% in S-CNWs. The OC-O bond contributes to the excellent water solubility of L-CDs, while S-CNWs have hydrophobicity due to the fewer OC-O bonds and more C-F bonds [45,46]. Moreover, the higher doping content of F and N in S-CNWs increases a lot of surface defects and the proportion of disordered carbon domains, which is consistent with the analysis results of FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of L-CDs and S-CNWs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties were explored by UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3a, the black line shows two typical UV-Vis absorption peaks of L-CDs in the high-energy region at 270 and 330 nm, attributed to the aromatic π→π∗ transition of the CC bond and the n→π∗ transition of the CO/CN bond in the carbon nucleus, respectively. And the weak absorption band in the low-energy region at 360–400 nm may be caused by the n→π∗ transition of the C-F bond [21,47]. The inset of Fig. 3a shows that the L-CDs solution is a pale-yellow liquid in sunlight and fluoresces strongly cyan under UV at 365 nm with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 420 nm and 467 nm, respectively (Fig. 3a, red and blue lines). Fig. 3b shows the intensity of the emission peak increases continuously at excitation wavelengths from 400 to 420 nm and then decreases gradually in the range of 420–450 nm. Significantly, the normalized fluorescence spectra of L-CDs in Fig. S3 displays that the emission peaks gradually red-shifted from 461 nm to 486 nm when the excitation wavelength is from 400 nm to 450 nm. These indicate that the excitation dependence of L-CDs is related to the generation of trap states of different energy levels by their surface functional groups [48]. The CIE coordinates of the best emission are (0.15, 0.27) (Fig. 3c), further proving the cyan emission of L-CDs. Next, the obtained yellow solid powder S-CNWs show yellow fluorescence with the best excitation and emission peak at 470 and 552 nm (Fig. 3d, red and blue lines). Compared with L-CDs, the significant red shift may be due to the narrowing of the band gap by the high content of F doping [49]. S-CNWs powder has a broad UV absorption peak at 250–400 nm, which may be caused by the π→π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds and n→π∗ transition of CO/CN/C-F. And a weak broad absorption in 450–600 nm, belongs to surface absorption of the S-CNWs and its subsequent excitation of the fluorescence emission [50,51] (Fig. 3d, black line). Most notably, F can ameliorate the aggregation-induced quenching phenomenon, which is the key to making solid-state S-CNWs luminescent. In Fig. 3e, the unchanged emission peak position indicates the excitation independence of S-CNW powder with only one luminous center. The optimal CIE coordinate in Fig. 3f is (0.40, 0.57), indicating the yellow light emission of S-CNW powder. Furthermore, the fluorescence decay curves of L-CDs solution and S-CNWs powder were also measured (Fig. S4a and b), and the results are 6.4 ns and 2.5 ns, respectively. It may be that π→π stacking interaction reduces the fluorescence emission of S-CNWs powder and makes its fluorescence lifetime lower than L-CDs solution [52].

Astonishingly, S-CNWs are dispersed in an aqueous solution as carbon nanowires (S-CNWs/H2O), but the dispersed state is disrupted in ethanol solution (S-CNWs/Et) as shown in Fig. S5a and b, which may be due to the stronger hydrogen bonding force in the aqueous solution. S-CNWs/H2O and S-CNWs/Et reveal UV absorption peaks at 270 and 330 nm similar to those of L-CDs and displayed blue and cyan fluorescence respectively under UV light. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of S-CNWs/H2O and S-CNWs/Et are 365, 439 nm and 409, 463 nm respectively (Fig. S6a and d). Owing to the small particle size and higher aggregation per unit range, the optimal emission of S-CNWs/Et is red-shifted compared to that of S-CNWs/H2O [53]. Fig. S6b presents the emission spectra of S-CNWs/H2O in the excitation wavelength range of 340–400 nm, showing an excitation-dependent behavior. And the optimal CIE coordinates are (0.15, 0.08) with blue fluorescence (Fig. S6c). Unlike the fluorescence spectra of S-CNWs/H2O, the S-CNWs/Et emission peak hardly changes throughout the excitation range of 370–440 nm, showing a weak excitation dependence (Fig. S6e), and the CIE coordinates are (0.15, 0.20), which is typical of cyan fluorescence (Fig. S6f).

L-CDs solution and PVA, two substances that do not have phosphorescent emission, were mixed and dried to obtain an L-CDs@PVA composite with RTP, and the optical properties of L-CDs@PVA were investigated. In Fig. 4a, the optimal excitation and emission of fluorescence for L-CDs@PVA are 390 and 437 nm, and the optimal excitation and emission of phosphorescence are 350 and 453 nm. A small stokes shift (16 nm) is generated between fluorescence and phosphorescence, indicating a small energy loss between S1 and T1 (ΔEst=0.10 eV). It favors ISC generating more triplet-state excitons to form phosphorescence emission. In addition, the fluorescence emission intensity of L-CDs@PVA undergoes an enhancement and then weakens in the excitation wavelength range of 350–390 nm and 390–420 nm (Fig. 4b), possessing excitation dependence with an emission peak shift of 16 nm (Fig. S7). It must be noted that the excitation dependence of L-CDs@PVA is weaker than L-CDs, indicating a narrower energy level distribution of surface state defects in L-CDs@PVA. The optimal CIE coordinate of L-CDs@PVA (Fig. 4c) appears at (0.16, 0.10) in the cyan fluorescence emission region. Fig. 4d exhibits single-peak emission under 310–360 nm excitation and broad double-peak emission in the excitation range of 370–400 nm, indicating the existence of two phosphorescence centers in L-CDs@PVA. The maximum of the double-phosphor band appears at 453 and 490 nm, and the double-peak emission occurs only at excitations greater than 360 nm. Moreover, L-CDs@PVA have a longer phosphorescence lifetime of 584.51 ms (Fig. 4e) compared to the already reported PVA-based RTP materials (Table S3).

By analysis of the structural and optical properties of L-CDs, we obtained the mechanism of RTP generation by L-CDs@PVA (Fig. 4f). The electrons in the ground state of L-CDs absorb the energy of the UV light and transition to the excited state. And after internal conversion and vibrational relaxation, electrons fall from Sn to the lowest singly excited state S1. When the electron returns from S1 to S0, energy is released in the form of light, producing fluorescence. However, heteroatomic functional groups (C-N, CN) are formed on the surface of L-CDs due to the doping of F and N atoms. These functional groups combined with lone pairs of electrons can promote the electron transition of n→π∗, and the energy gap (ΔEst) between the singlet and triplet states also reduces due to the doping of F atoms [32,54,55]. These make it easy for some of the singlet excitons in S1 to reach the lowest triplet excited state T1 by ISC and increase the collisions of triplet excitons. The light emitted by the triplet excitons return to the ground state S0 is phosphorescence. It is worth noting that phosphorescence is easily quenched by oxygen. Therefore, the polymer long chains of PVA and the abundant hydrogen bonds formed between PVA and L-CDs are important for the protection of the phosphorescent centers throughout the luminescence process. The rigid environment effectively insulates atmospheric oxygen, inhibits intramolecular motion, and weakens the nonradiative transition of triplet-state excitons [56]. Moreover, the doped F atoms (half-ionic C-F bond) improve the hydrogen bonding interaction and facilitates the stable emission of RTP [21].

In this era of data transparency, information security is crucial, which can minimize our losses in economic and other aspects. The potential applications of L-CD and S-CNWs in information encryption were explored. In real life, it can be used for the encryption of important documents and the anti-counterfeiting of books and banknotes. The L-CDs@PVA gels were printed on filter paper with patterns of QR codes, letters, and school logos as in Fig. 5a and Fig. S8. No patterns could be observed under daylight, but these patterns showed cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. After removing the UV light source, they were able to continue to emit light and the green phosphorescence could be observed by the naked eye for more than 1 s. Due to the different optical behavior of L-CDs and S-CNWs, that is, both L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA have cyan fluorescence under UV light, but only L-CDs@PVA has a phosphorescence phenomenon after turning off the UV light, so combining the optical phenomena of them, we designed the multi-level encryption method. As shown in Fig. 5b, the letters "Q, I, D, O" and "N, G, A" were printed on the filter paper with L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA gels by screen printing, respectively. Under natural light, the filter paper showed no information, while under UV light, the word "QING DAO" showed clearly with cyan fluorescence. Furthermore, after the UV light was turned off, the filter paper conveyed different information content, and "QI DO" could be seen clearing with green phosphorescence. We also produced a message storage pattern in a standard 8-bit ASCII character binary code (Fig. 5c). According to the designed code, red and blue spots represented 0 and 1, corresponding to S-CNWs@PVA and L-CDs@PVA printed on the filter paper. When the 365 nm UV light was on, all spots show cyan fluorescence and no valid information could be read. After removing the UV source, the spots labeled with L-CDs@PVA emitted green phosphorescence, while the spots labeled with S-CNWs@PVA emitted no phosphorescence, which allowed the exposed information to be easily converted into the binary codes "01010100", "01010010", and "01001001", corresponding to the letter "Q", "D", and "U ". This sophisticated encryption is difficult to break, making the information significantly more secure. More interestingly, the phosphorescence of L-CDs@PVA is characterized by quenching under humid conditions. When we applied S-CNWs@PVA and L-CDs@PVA on the red and blue marks respectively according to the designed "8888" number (Fig. 5d) and printed on the filter paper, the number on the filter paper could not be observed by naked eyes under daylight. While the cyan fluorescent "8888" was displayed on the filter paper under UV light, the really useful information "5946" could be seen clearly when the UV light was turned off. Furthermore, when water was sprayed on the filter paper, the phosphorescence information "5946" disappeared completely, and the fluorescence information "8888" remained. More importantly, after drying the filter paper again, the information "5946" with phosphorescence emission appeared again with good reproducibility. For the characteristics of stability of fluorescence in dry and wet environments and reproducibility of phosphorescence, the application of L-CDs@PVA and S-CNWs@PVA in multi-level information encryption systems is extended. Additionally, since the cyan fluorescence of L-CDs@PVA is the same color as the fluorescence of commercial printing paper, L-CDs@PVA was used as ink to draw Morse code "dot" and "horizontal line" (Fig. S9). The designed Morse code could not be seen under sunlight and 365 nm UV light, but only when the UV light source was removed, the Morse code with green phosphorescent emission could be seen, and the code could be easily decrypted into "Q, D, U", and the message was successfully encrypted and transmitted through the Morse code. Miraculously, we also found that the fluorescence color of S-CNWs powder has a great difference under different conditions of dryness and humidity. As shown in Fig. 5e, the dried S-CNWs powder with the message "BINGO" was printed on a smooth glass substrate and emitted yellow fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. After spraying the glass plate with water, the fluorescence of "BINGO" changed to blue under UV light, and then changed to the original yellow emission after drying, which is reproducible and an interesting fluorescence color change phenomenon. Moreover, because of the unique solid-state fluorescence emission of S-CNWs powder, the S-CNWs powder in fingerprint visualization was further investigated. Fingerprints printed on glass plates treated with S-CNWs powder had a clear yellow fluorescence imaging under UV light (Fig. 5f), and detailed information about sweat pores, bifurcations, and short ridges[57] could be observed clearly in the magnified image, which plays an important role in the identification of individuals and has potential for practical application.

In summary, F, N co-doped carbon dots (L-CDs) and carbon nanowires (S-CNWs) with different optical behaviors were successfully prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method using enrofloxacin. The L-CDs@PVA composite not only has cyan fluorescence but also has green RTP with a phosphorescence lifetime of 584.51 ms, which is closely related to the surface modification of L-CDs by F and N atoms. C-N, CN bonding with lone pair electrons promotes the electron leap of n→π∗, and F doping reduces the energy gap from single to triplet states, making the system more prone to ISC processes and increasing the radiative leap of triplet excitons. As well, PVA forms an abundant hydrogen bond with L-CDs to effectively fix and protect the luminescence center, which is also the key factor for L-CDs@PVA to produce phosphorescence stably at room temperature. More interestingly, unlike L-CDs, the S-CNWs possess solid-state yellow fluorescence. And it has distinctly different fluorescent colors under dry and moist conditions and is bi-directionally convertible. In contrast, only cyan fluorescence emission without phosphorescence phenomenon was observed for S-CNWs@PVA. Thus, by cleverly exploiting the similarities and differences in the luminescence phenomena of L-CDs and S-CNWs, a hard-to-break multi-level information encryption method was successfully designed with potential applications.

Hongmei Zhang: Experiment, analysis, Data collation, Writing – original draft. Xiangjun Guo: Experiment, Formal analysis, Data sorting. Jiyao Xu: Sort out data and analyze. Yuman Zhang: Data sorting and analyze. Yujia Deng: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xihui Zhao: Review and revise the article, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR202110280005), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21972071), and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and EcoTextiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.168910.
